chunk_summary: Swallowed topical corticosteroids, such as fluticasone and budesonide, are effective for managing pediatric eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE) when administered correctly. Proper technique, safety monitoring, and follow-up endoscopy are essential to ensure treatment success and minimize adverse effects.
learning_objectives: Understand the correct administration techniques for fluticasone and budesonide in pediatric EoE.
Recognize the importance of safety monitoring, including oral candidiasis prevention and endocrine evaluation.
Learn the role of endoscopy and biopsies in confirming treatment response in EoE.
Identify common pitfalls in steroid administration and strategies to improve adherence.
key_facts: Fluticasone should be sprayed into the mouth and swallowed without a spacer, avoiding food or drink for 30 minutes post-administration.
Budesonide is administered as an oral viscous slurry, avoiding honey in infants.
Oral or esophageal candidiasis is the most common adverse effect; families should rinse and spit after dosing.
Prolonged high-dose steroid use can suppress the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis; consider endocrine review and morning cortisol testing in at-risk children.
Endoscopy and biopsies are recommended after 8–12 weeks of induction therapy to confirm histologic remission.
Improper administration, such as inhaling fluticasone, can lead to treatment failure.
reference_quote: Swallowed topical corticosteroids are an effective induction and maintenance option for pediatric EoE, but outcomes depend on correct administration technique. (ESPGHAN, 2024)
document_key_topics: Swallowed topical steroid administration in EoE
Safety monitoring and adverse effects of corticosteroids
Role of endoscopy in EoE treatment response
